next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Joel Beckman had been counting the hours since anyone last asked him about the argument. Fourteen. A personal best for this weekend, though the bar was roughly ankle-high. The previous record had been six hours, overnight, because even these people had to sleep eventually.

He sat at the kitchen table with a turkey sandwich he wasn't eating and watched Nate try to scrape the last of the mustard from the jar with a butter knife. Cliff was at the counter cutting an apple into mathematically precise slices. Tommy was on his second sandwich, or possibly his third — Joel had stopped tracking. Paul sat across from Joel with a cup of coffee he kept warming in the microwave, the same cup he'd been nursing since morning, which Joel found either disciplined or insane.

Doug was eating a bowl of cereal, because Doug had apparently decided that cereal was a valid Sunday lunch option, and nobody had the energy to argue.

The chair at the head of the table was empty. Nobody had mentioned it. Everyone had noticed.

"Mustard's done," Nate announced, holding up the jar. "We're officially post-mustard. I want that noted for the record."

"There's more in the garage fridge," Paul said.

"The garage fridge. Of course. Because Martin had a condiment annex." Nate set the jar down and took a bite of his dry sandwich. He chewed for a while. "His garage fridge had better beer than my actual fridge. That's not a bit. That's just true."

The kitchen was quiet except for the refrigerator's cycling hum and Tommy's methodical chewing. Afternoon light came through the windows at a low angle,...
open_hooks_to_carry: Cliff's financial motive: Martin owed him forty thousand from a failed joint real estate deal
The safe: wall-mounted, combination lock, Martin left the dial one tick off
The manila envelope labeled RT — Cliff expects real estate documents
Martin's handwritten account of the 2002 cabin fire in Washburn County, Wisconsin
The account names Tommy as the one who started the fire; names Ray Toomey as the caretaker who died
Cliff reads it twice, puts it back, and the reader should feel the temperature change
prev_tail_excerpt:
...shaken hands and made small talk and eaten nothing and slept poorly and watched his friend die from a distance of one floor and the length of a hallway, and he was still here. On a dock. Watching minnows. Sitting in September light that made the lake look like something worth coming back to.

That had to count for something.

The wind picked up slightly, pushing small ripples across the surface. Doug pulled the sleeves of his hoodie over his hands and watched the ripples reach the dock and flatten against the pilings. He thought about going inside and decided against it and then kept deciding against it as the sun moved and the gold went amber and the amber went gray, which was a color he knew well.

When the light was mostly gone he went inside, ate a piece of the sourdough toast Cliff had left on the counter, and took the basement stairs down to the pull-out sofa. He lay there in the dark and listened to the refrigerator cycle above him, its asthmatic hum kicking on, running, clicking off, the forty-minute rhythm of a machine that had been keeping Martin's food cold for longer than Doug had known Martin. He didn't sleep. He didn't cry. He didn't do anything at all except exist in a house full of men who were hiding things, his own hidden thing the smallest and most ordinary of the lot — two pill bottles in a duffel bag, and a marriage that had ended, and the quiet fact of being alive when he wasn't sure what for, which wasn't a thing a detective would ask about and wasn't a thing his friends would notice and wasn't a thing that mattered to anyone but him, and only barely.
secondary_character_beats: Cliff's financial panic should be legible as genuine desperation, not greed — his business is failing and this money matters

setups_to_plant.description: The handwritten account of the 2002 fire — Martin's record of Tommy starting the fire that killed Ray Toomey. Cliff finds it, photographs it, and replaces it. This document drives the murder motive and the resolution.

state_deltas_required: Cliff's financial desperation driving him to Martin's safe, where he finds something far more valuable than money
Cliff holds the most dangerous secret in the house — leverage that he does not yet understand is lethal
